home.subtitle: Open-source network observability from probes you control.
home.introBefore: Netstamp is an open-source, self-hosted network observability app that helps measure
home.introAfter: and incidents from the viewpoints that matter.
home.showcaseTitle: A green dot is not enough.
home.showcaseDescription: Netstamp connects the current state to the evidence behind it.
showcase: See every reporting location, its health, and the network view it contributes.
Inspect round-trip time, packet loss, success rate, and long-running trends.
Break requests into DNS, connect, TLS, TTFB, and total response timing.
Verify whether critical ports are reachable from every assigned probe.
Compare route graphs, hop latency, path loss, and changes over time.
Alert before certificate expiry becomes an avoidable production incident.
Turn threshold breaches into focused incidents with rules and notifications.
Publish live component health and recent history without exposing the console.
home.flowTitle: From real probes to real incidents.
faqs: Is Netstamp just another uptime monitor?

No. Uptime is only one layer. Netstamp combines real-world probes, ping, TCP reachability, traceroute, HTTP uptime, TLS certificate checks, packet loss, latency, probe health, incidents, dashboards, and historical measurements so you can understand how a service behaves from different networks.
Why do I need probes I control?

Because third-party cloud checks cannot see every network that matters to you. A service may be reachable from a public monitoring region but degraded from your office, lab, ISP, edge node, private network, or user-facing environment. Netstamp gives you visibility from those real viewpoints.

Yes. Netstamp is open-source and designed for self-hosted deployments with a controller, lightweight probes, PostgreSQL, TimescaleDB, health checks, metrics, OpenAPI, and production deployment documentation.

Netstamp supports real-world network checks such as ping, TCP, traceroute, HTTP uptime, API payload checks, and TLS/SSL certificate monitoring. It also tracks probe health, latest measurements, incidents, dashboards, and historical trends.

Netstamp is for developers, infrastructure teams, homelab users, SREs, network operators, researchers, and teams that need to observe services from machines, regions, ISPs, labs, edge nodes, private infrastructure, or real user-facing networks they control.
How does Netstamp help with debugging?

Netstamp helps you separate global outages from regional, provider-specific, probe-specific, target-specific, and route-specific problems. Historical measurements make it easier to see what changed, where it changed, and when it started.
home.finalTitle: Start observing from the networks you control.
